title: Fake News, Foucault, and Durkheim: Trump's Term Has Prophetic Roots
teaser: Émile Durkheim published The Rules of Sociological Method in 1895. Michel Foucault delivered 'The Order of Discourse' in 1970. Donald Trump used the phrase 'fake news' 2,019 documented times during his first term. This article does not argue that Durkheim and Foucault predicted Trump. It argues that they described the mechanism that makes the phrase work, with precision that 130 years of political history has only confirmed. The Power of Words in Everyday Life.
verifiedSource: Foucault, Power/Knowledge (Pantheon, 1980); Durkheim, The Rules of Sociological Method (1895); Bourdieu, Language and Symbolic Power (Harvard UP, 1991); Merriam-Webster Dictionary (fake news, first use ~1890)
body:
Émile Durkheim published The Rules of Sociological Method in 1895. Michel Foucault delivered “The Order of Discourse” as his inaugural lecture at the Collège de France in 1970. Donald Trump used the phrase “fake news” 2,019 documented times during his first term in office. This article does not argue that Durkheim and Foucault predicted Trump. It argues that they described the mechanism that makes the phrase work, with a precision that 130 years of political history has only confirmed. Knowing the mechanism is not the same as knowing the politics. The mechanism operates independently of the politics. That is precisely what makes it analytically significant.

Foucault and the Regime of Truth

In 1980, Colin Gordon edited a collection of Foucault’s interviews and essays into English under the title Power/Knowledge. The central analytical concept in that volume is what Foucault called the “regime of truth”: the ensemble of procedures, norms, and institutions within a society that determines what can be counted as knowledge, and who holds the authority to produce it.

Foucault identified that each society operates a “general politics of truth”: the types of discourse it accepts as functioning as true. The mechanism is precise. Truth is not discovered; it is produced. Production requires institutional authority. Courts produce legal truth. Laboratories produce scientific truth. Newsrooms, historically, produced journalistic truth. Each institution operates under a regime that grants or withholds the status of legitimate knowledge from the claims it circulates.

Foucault’s insight is not that truth is relative. It is that the social category of “knowledge” requires an institution to hold and certify it. The content of any particular claim matters less than whether the institutional structure backing it is accepted as legitimate by the audience that receives the claim.

This is the mechanism that makes “fake news” function as a political instrument rather than a content critique. The phrase is not a refutation. It contains no argument and no counter-evidence. It is a delegitimization move: an attempt to withdraw the institutional authority of a journalism organization from the claims that organization makes. Once the institution is delegitimized, the specific claim does not need to be addressed. The audience has already been given permission to discount it.

Foucault’s regime of truth framework predicts this operation with uncomfortable precision. The political actor who can successfully challenge the epistemic authority of rival truth-producing institutions acquires a structural advantage that functions independently of any particular factual dispute. The dispute over a specific story becomes secondary to the dispute over who holds the authority to produce reality for a given audience.

Durkheim and the Social Fact

Durkheim’s 1895 definition of a social fact is formal: a way of acting, thinking, or feeling that is external to the individual and exercises coercive power over them. A social fact does not need to be empirically true to function sociologically. It needs to be collectively believed.

Durkheim extended this concept to what he called “collective representations”: ideas that a group holds in common and that function as social reality. A collective representation is real in its effects regardless of its relationship to empirical fact. This is what Durkheim meant by the social reality of beliefs: not that they are true, but that they produce real social consequences when they are widely held by a group that enforces them on its members.

When a political figure with the institutional visibility of a sitting president repeats the phrase “fake news” 2,019 times across a four-year term, the phrase becomes a collective representation. It acquires social force. The empirical question of whether any particular news item is accurate becomes sociologically secondary to the social function the phrase performs as a binding collective belief within the audience that accepts it.

What Durkheim’s framework identifies is the predictable product of this operation: a split epistemic community. Two groups share the same informational environment and the same news events, but hold incompatible frameworks for evaluating the truth-status of what they encounter. One group accepts the institutional authority of established news organizations. The other has been given a collective representation that functions as a standing permission to reject that authority. This is not irrationality. It is exactly the outcome Durkheim’s sociology predicts when competing collective representations operate on the same social field.

The Historical Receipt: 1890

Merriam-Webster places the first documented use of the phrase “fake news” at approximately 1890, in the context of American press criticism of yellow journalism. The phrase is at minimum 130 years old. The dispute it described in 1890 had the same structural form as the dispute in 2016: established journalism institutions labeling sensationalist competitors as fraudulent, while sensationalist papers positioned themselves as representatives of authentic public interests against the pretensions of elite media.

William Randolph Hearst and Joseph Pulitzer built circulations in the millions through exactly the epistemic strategy that Foucault would later theorize. The institutional authority of established newspapers was challenged by papers that positioned themselves as advocates for the common reader against a corrupt establishment press. The establishment press responded by calling the challenger press “fake.” The challenger press called the establishment press biased and out of touch. Audiences split along epistemic lines.

What changed between 1895 and 2016 is not the mechanism. What changed is the amplification infrastructure. In 1895, a phrase repeated in a single newspaper reached a city. In 2016, a phrase repeated on Twitter reached a network of 80 million followers within seconds. Foucault and Durkheim were theorizing a mechanism that existed in full form before the amplification technology existed. The technology accelerated the mechanism’s effects. It did not invent the mechanism.

Bourdieu and Symbolic Power

A third scholar completes the analytical frame. Pierre Bourdieu’s 1991 collection Language and Symbolic Power introduced the concept of “symbolic violence”: the imposition of systems of meaning that serve the interests of dominant groups, carried out with the willing acceptance of those systems by the groups it subordinates. Symbolic violence does not require physical coercion. It requires only that the subordinated group accept the legitimacy of the categories through which the subordination operates.

Applied to the “fake news” operation: the phrase functions as symbolic violence not against any individual journalist, but against the collective practice of institutional journalism as a social category. When the phrase succeeds, it trains audiences to experience a feeling of recognition and permission when they encounter journalism that challenges power. They do not need to evaluate the specific claim. The category “fake news” has already performed the evaluative work for them. The effect, as Bourdieu would predict, reliably serves the interests of whoever issued the category.

What distinguishes this from ordinary political contestation is the precision with which the phrase targets the institutional layer rather than the content layer. Political actors have always disputed specific news stories. Calling a story false is content-level contestation. Calling an institution “fake” is institutional-level contestation. Bourdieu’s framework identifies why the institutional-level attack is structurally more powerful: it does not need to win any individual dispute. It only needs to create persistent doubt about whether the institution has the authority to produce reality for its audience.

What the Scholarship Requires Us to Conclude

Three analytical claims follow from the scholarship, stated without political framing:

First: “Fake news” as a political phrase is not a content critique. It is an institutional delegitimization strategy. This is a structural observation, not a partisan one. Any political actor with sufficient institutional visibility and the capacity for repetition could deploy the same mechanism. The political valence of any particular deployment is distinct from the mechanism itself.

Second: The sociological effectiveness of the phrase is independent of its accuracy in any individual application. This is Durkheim’s point. Social facts function through collective belief, not through empirical verification. Whether a specific news story is accurate or inaccurate is analytically separable from whether the phrase functions as a social fact in the community hearing it.

Third: The phrase has historical precedent and will have historical successors. The mechanism is not unique to any political moment or political actor. What varies across historical eras is the technology that amplifies it. The scholars who described this mechanism were not making a political argument. They were building a theory of how power and knowledge interact in modern societies. Their work remains the most precise analytical framework available for explaining why a two-word phrase can function as a significant instrument of political power.

The receipt is in the primary literature. ClownBinge files it under NerdOut.
